alsoKnownAs: Red Devil Cichlid (trade overlap with A. labiatus)

nativeRange.habitat: The Midas Cichlid is native to large lakes and their associated river systems in Central America, primarily Lake Nicaragua and Lake Managua. In the wild it occupies shallow to mid-depth areas — reaching down to around 15 metres — along rocky shorelines and sandy littoral zones, typically over hard substrate with scattered vegetation.

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and rock formations for territory delineation
thick sandy substrate (10+ cm) for spawning pit excavation
minimal or no live plants (will be uprooted and destroyed)
PVC or ceramic caves as shelter and breeding sites
robust driftwood for additional territory markers

diet.notes: The Midas Cichlid is an opportunistic omnivore in the wild, consuming molluscs, crustaceans, insects, plant matter, algae, and detritus. In captivity it does well on high-quality large cichlid pellets as a staple, supplemented with frozen foods such as krill, shrimp, earthworms, and bloodworm, along with occasional vegetable matter such as blanched zucchini or peas. Live feeder fish are generally best avoided, as they are associated with disease transmission and nutritional imbalance. Feeding once or twice daily is recommended; the species tends toward obesity if overfed.

goodWith: bonded mate of the same species (established pair only)
similarly sized robust Central American cichlids in very large tanks (with caution)
large armoured catfish (common pleco, sailfin pleco) as dither/tank-mate in adequately sized setups
avoidWith: all small or medium-sized fish (will be killed or eaten)
other cichlids unless in extremely large tanks with territory breaks
fish with long flowing fins (will be attacked)
conspecifics outside a bonded pair (severe aggression)
any other Amphilophus or Vieja species (hybridisation risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo of Amphilophus citrinellus; shows typical wild-type dark-barred juvenile/sub-adult coloration.
Clear lateral photograph of adult gold morph male with developed nuchal hump; well-lit, good resolution. Recommended candidate.
SeriouslyFish species profile image; good quality but copyrighted — use Wikimedia candidate instead.

summary: The Midas Cichlid is a large, aggressive Central American cichlid reaching 35 cm, with adult males developing a prominent bony cranial hump. It occurs in wild-type barred and vivid gold-orange color forms, and is one of the parent species behind the artificial Flowerhorn hybrid. A species-only tank of 500 L or more is strongly advised.
careNotes:
The Midas Cichlid is widely considered one of the largest and most aggressive of the Neotropical cichlids. Adult males can attain 35 cm total length and develop a pronounced nuchal hump — a bony cranial protrusion colloquially called a 'kok' — that enlarges with age and condition; gold and orange color morphs in particular are prized in the hobby. A minimum tank volume of 500 L with at least 180 cm footprint is required for a single adult or a bonded pair. The tank should be heavily filtered with robust biological and mechanical filtration, as Midas Cichlids are voracious eaters that produce a substantial waste load. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are advisable.

The species is widely regarded as highly territorial and destructive: all décor (rocks, PVC, wood) must be heavy and well-secured as substrate excavation and rearranging of tank furniture is near-constant. Live plants are generally not suitable — plastic or silk alternatives are the practical option if greenery is desired. Sand substrate of at least 10 cm depth accommodates natural pit-digging during spawning and general foraging behaviour.

Midas Cichlids are typically kept as a single specimen or a single bonded pair. A bonded male and female tends to defend their territory with extreme intensity, attacking anything that enters it, including the keeper's hand during water changes. Tankmates are generally not recommended; a few very large armoured catfish (Pterygoplichthys) may be tolerated in very large tanks but are still at risk during breeding periods.

The species readily hybridises with related Amphilophus and Vieja species; A. citrinellus crossed with A. trimaculatus is documented as one of the founding lineages of the artificially produced Flowerhorn (see [fw-fish-348]). Midas Cichlids should not be kept with other Amphilophus or Vieja species unless deliberately breeding hybrids is the intent.

The trade name 'Red Devil' is frequently misapplied to both A. citrinellus (Midas Cichlid) and A. labiatus (True Red Devil Cichlid). The Midas is typically rounder-bodied with a larger nuchal hump at maturity, while A. labiatus has pronounced fleshy lips. When purchasing, verifying the species with the vendor is worthwhile where possible.
breedingNotes:
The Midas Cichlid is an open substrate-spawner. A bonded pair typically excavates a flat pit or selects a flat rock surface as a spawning site, cleaning it meticulously before eggs are deposited. Females lay 1,000–2,000 adhesive eggs in a typical clutch, with the male fertilising immediately after. Both parents share intensive brood care: eggs are fanned continuously, unfertilised eggs are removed, and newly hatched wrigglers are moved to pre-dug pits in the substrate. Both parents tend to defend the fry fiercely against all intruders for 4–6 weeks until the juveniles are free-swimming and foraging independently.

Breeding is most reliably achieved by introducing a group of juveniles and allowing them to pair naturally; forcing together unacquainted adults frequently results in the death of one fish. A tank divider is sometimes used when introducing adult specimens for the first time. Pairs commonly become more aggressive toward the keeper during spawning periods, and heavy gloves are advisable during maintenance. Fry accept finely crushed cichlid pellets and micro-worms once free-swimming. Juveniles should be removed before the parents begin preparation for the next spawn cycle, as they may be attacked.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Amphilophus citrinellus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to 30 cm, females smaller), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 5–20 dH, native range (Lake Nicaragua, Lake Managua, associated rivers in Nicaragua and Costa Rica), biotope, diet (omnivore), spawning behaviour (open substrate-spawner, biparental care), and compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Amphilophus citrinellus
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum total length (35 cm TL males), native range (Nicaragua, Costa Rica), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), depth range, temperature (20–28°C), diet (omnivore — invertebrates, plant material). FishBase maximum size of 35 cm used per brief.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Midas Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide confirming 500 L+ tank requirement, high aggression, species-only recommended, diet (pellets, frozen, earthworms), and nuchal hump development in males. Noted gold color morph as the classic 'Midas' form.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Midas Cichlid
additional.notes: Supplemental source confirming habitat (rocky lakeside and sandy zones, Lake Nicaragua and Lake Managua), biparental substrate-spawning behaviour, colour polymorphism (dark wild-type vs gold morph), and Flowerhorn hybrid parentage. Aggression level and 500 L+ minimum tank size corroborated.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Central American Cichlids
additional.notes: TFH historical coverage of A. citrinellus; confirmed colour polymorphism genetics, nuchal hump ('kok') development and hormonal basis, relationship to Flowerhorn hybrids (A. citrinellus × A. trimaculatus lineage), and long captive lifespan (10–15 years documented in optimal conditions).

fish.breedingNotes: The Midas Cichlid is an open substrate-spawner that deposits clutches of 1,000–2,000 eggs on flat rocks or in excavated substrate pits. Both parents share intense brood care for approximately 4–6 weeks. The most reliable way to obtain a compatible pair is to raise a group of juveniles together and allow a bond to form naturally, as forcing together unacquainted adults frequently results in the death of one fish. When introducing adult specimens, a tank divider is generally recommended during the initial period.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: The Midas Cichlid reaches up to 35 cm and is widely considered one of the most aggressive of the Central American cichlids. A species-only setup of 500 L or more is typically required, with most keepers housing a single specimen or a bonded pair. The species hybridises readily with related Amphilophus and Vieja cichlids and is documented as one of the founding lineages of the Flowerhorn hybrid ([fw-fish-348]).
